perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise expressly authorized by t he disclosing party, the receiving party agrees to retain the “Confidential Information” in confidence for a period of three (3) years from the date of receipt of the “Confidential Information” or until such time as the information no longer qualifies as “ Confidential Information” under Paragraph 3.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise expressly authorized by t he disclosing party, the receiving party agrees to retain the “Confidential Information” in confidence for a period of one (1) year from the date of receipt of the “Confidential Information” or until such time as the information no longer qualifies as “ Confidential Information” under Paragraph 3.
perturbation.explanation: The change modifies the confidentiality period to one year. Under Florida's statute of limitations for breach of contract, this is problematic because the statute provides up to five years to bring such an action, depending on whether the contract is written or oral. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year effectively reduces the time a party has to pursue legal action for a breach of confidentiality that occurred during that initial year.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 95.11 - Limitations other than for the recovery of real property
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 95.11 sets forth the statute of limitations for various actions. Specifically, a legal or equitable action on a contract, obligation, or liability founded on a written instrument must be commenced within five years. By limiting the confidentiality period to one year, the contract attempts to shorten the period during which a breach of confidentiality claim can be brought, conflicting with the statutory limitations period.

F.S. 95.11 95.11 Limitations other than for the recovery of real property. — Actions other than for recovery of real property shall be commenced as follows: (1) WITHIN TWENTY YEARS. — An action on a judgment or decree of a court of record in this state. (2) WITHIN FIVE YEARS. — (a) An action on a judgment or decree of any court, not of record, of this state or any court of the United States, any other state or territory in the United States, or a foreign country. (b) A legal or equitable action on a contract, obligation, or liability founded on a written instrument, except for an action to enforce a claim against a payment bond, which shall be governed by the applicable provisions of paragraph (5)(e), s. 255.05 (10), s. 337.18 (1), or s. 713.23 (1)(e), and except for an action for a deficiency judgment governed by paragraph (5)(h). (c) An action to foreclose a mortgage. (d) An action alleging a willful violation of s. 448.110 . (e) Notwithstanding paragraph (b), an action for breach of a property insurance contract, with the period running from the date of loss. 1 (3) WITHIN FOUR YEARS. — (a) An action relating to the determination of paternity, with the time running from the date the child reaches the age of majority. 2 (b) An action founded on the design, planning, or construction of an improvement to real property, with the time running from the date the authority having jurisdiction issues a temporary certificate of occupancy, a certificate of occupancy, or a certificate of completion, or the date of abandonment of construction if not completed, whichever date is earliest; except that, when the action involves a latent defect, the time runs from the time the defect is discovered or should have been discovered with the exercise of due diligence. In any event, the action must be commenced within 7 years after the date the authority having jurisdiction issues a temporary certificate of occupancy, a certificate of occupancy, or a certificate of completion, or the date of aban
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year, while Florida Statute 95.11(2)(b) establishes a five-year statute of limitations for actions on a contract founded on a written instrument. This creates a legal contradiction because the contractual term attempts to shorten the period during which a breach of confidentiality claim can be brought, conflicting with the five-year statutory limitations period. The scraped snippet from Chapter 95 Section 11 of the 2023 Florida Statutes supports this, explicitly stating that a legal action on a written contract must commence within five years.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this agreement at any time by giving written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this agreement at any time by giving verbal notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows for verbal termination of the agreement, which could be problematic under the Statute of Frauds if the agreement falls under its purview. The Statute of Frauds generally requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 725.01 - Promise to pay debt of another; requirement of writing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 725.01, also known as the Statute of Frauds, requires certain agreements to be in writing and signed to be enforceable. Although a standard NDA may not always fall under the statute, making the termination verbal creates ambiguity. If the agreement involves matters that trigger the Statute of Frauds (e.g., contracts that cannot be performed within one year), a verbal termination would likely be unenforceable, contradicting the statute's requirement for a written agreement.

Entire Chapter SECTION 01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. F.S. 725.01 725.01 Promise to pay another’s debt, etc. — No action shall be brought whereby to charge any executor or administrator upon any special promise to answer or pay any debt or damages out of her or his own estate, or whereby to charge the defendant upon any special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person or to charge any person upon any agreement made upon consideration of marriage, or upon any contract for the sale of lands, tenements or hereditaments, or of any uncertain interest in or concerning them, or for any lease thereof for a period longer than 1 year, or upon any agreement that is not to be performed within the space of 1 year from the making thereof, or whereby to charge any health care provider upon any guarantee, warranty, or assurance as to the results of any medical, surgical, or diagnostic procedure performed by any physician licensed under chapter 458, osteopathic physician licensed under chapter 459, chiropractic physician licensed under chapter 460, podiatric physician licensed under chapter 461, or dentist licensed under chapter 466, unless the agreement or promise upon which such action shall be brought, or some note or memorandum thereof shall be in writing and signed by the party to be charged therewith or by some other person by her or him thereunto lawfully authorized. History. — s. 10, Nov. 15, 1828; RS 1995; GS 2517; RGS 3872; CGL 5779; s. 10, ch. 75-9; s. 933, ch. 97-102; s. 60, ch. 97-264; ss. 227, 294, ch. 98-166.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by allowing verbal termination, contradicts Florida Statute 725.01 (Statute of Frauds) as explained in both the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Statute 725.01, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires certain agreements, including those not performable within one year, to be in writing. If the agreement falls under this category, a verbal termination would be unenforceable, directly conflicting with the statutory requirement for a written agreement or memorandum. The original text requires written notice, thus adhering to the Statute of Frauds, while the changed text does not, potentially rendering the termination clause unenforceable. This creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: At the instruction of disclosing party, the receiving party agrees to either r eturn or dispose of all information received under this agreement, discontinue any use of the “Confidential Information”, and provide written confirmation thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: At the instruction of disclosing party, the receiving party agrees to either r eturn or dispose of all information received under this agreement, discontinue any use of the “Confidential Information”, and provide confirmation thereof within 90 days.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confirmation requirement to 'within 90 days' creates a potential conflict with trade secret laws. If the confidential information constitutes a trade secret under Florida law, a 90-day delay in confirming the return or disposal of the information could be deemed an unreasonable delay, especially if there's a risk of imminent misappropriation. The Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and a lengthy delay could be seen as a failure to take such measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 688.003 allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By allowing a 90 day window to confirm destruction of confidential information, it could be viewed as enabling further use of that information, thereby potentially violating the UTSA by not taking reasonable efforts to prevent misappropriation.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 90-day window for confirming the return or disposal of confidential information, which could be viewed as conflicting with the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets under Florida law. The Law Explanation argues that this delay could enable further use of the information, thereby violating the UTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing Florida Statute 688.003, allows for injunctive relief to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. The 90-day window could be interpreted as a failure to act promptly to prevent potential misappropriation, thus contradicting the legal obligation to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement and the legal relations between the parties shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of the State of Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement and the legal relations between the parties shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the law of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California introduces potential inconsistencies if the parties' principal places of business and the NDA's subject matter are primarily connected to Florida. While parties can generally agree on a choice of law, courts may refuse to enforce the chosen law if it has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and it contradicts a fundamental policy of the state whose law would otherwise apply (Florida).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 671.301 - Territorial applicability; parties' power to choose applicable law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 671.301(1) allows parties to choose the law that governs their agreement, but includes the caveat that when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties can agree that the law of either this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. Changing the jurisdiction to California is only enforceable if there is a reasonable relation to the state; there is nothing to suggest there is a connection to California, therefore Florida law may supersede that clause.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 671 > Section 301 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 671 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 301 Effective date; provision for transition; preservation of old transition provision. F.S. 671.301 671.301 Effective date; provision for transition; preservation of old transition provision. — (1) This act shall take effect at 12:01 a.m. on January 1, 1980. (2) Transactions validly entered into after January 1, 1967, and before the effective date of this act, and which were subject to the provisions of chapter 65-254, Laws of Florida, as amended, and which would be subject to this act as amended if they had been entered into after the effective date of this act and the rights, duties, and interests flowing from such transactions remain valid after the latter date and may be terminated, completed, consummated, or enforced as required or permitted by this act. Security interests arising out of such transactions which are perfected when this act becomes effective shall remain perfected until they lapse as provided in this act and may be continued as permitted by this act, except as stated in s. 680.108 , Florida Statutes 1979. (3) The provisions of s. 680.101 , Florida Statutes 1977, shall continue to apply to this act and for this purpose the two statutes shall be considered one continuous statute. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 1, ch. 69-218; s. 36, ch. 79-398; s. 6, ch. 90-278. Note. — Former s. 680.101.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law of the agreement from Florida to California. Florida Statute 671.301(1), as provided in the scraped snippet, allows parties to choose the governing law, but implies that the transaction must bear a reasonable relation to the chosen state. The perturbation explanation highlights the potential inconsistency if the agreement's subject matter and the parties involved are primarily connected to Florida. If there's no reasonable connection to California, applying California law could contradict Florida law, particularly if it affects fundamental policies. Thus, changing the jurisdiction to California creates a potential legal contradiction because it may be unenforceable under Florida law.
